data.title: Persistent Inflation Constrains Policy
data.description:
Transcript

We think the Middle East conflict only piles onto inflationary pressures already bubbling under the surface. Higher yields are here to stay, in our view. That means long-term government bonds are no longer effective diversifiers against equity market declines.

We think the Middle East conflict only piles onto inflationary pressures already bubbling under the surface. Higher yields are here to stay, in our view. That means long-term government bonds are no longer effective diversifiers against equity market declines.

1) Too-rosy view on inflation

Prior to the Middle East conflict, markets ignored signs that inflation’s downward trend had already stalled. Core services inflation remained stubbornly high. An aging population and immigration curbs make for a tight labor market. Add the AI-led capex boom and tariff-driven goods inflation, and it was clear to us that core inflation would stay hot.

Markets have embraced this view: They are pricing out U.S. rate cuts and expect the European Central Bank to raise interest rates this year instead of staying put.

2) A world shaped by supply

The big picture? We’re in a world shaped by supply, where supply constraints are the main drivers of inflation. So central banks face a stark trade-off between reining in inflation or supporting economic growth.

There’s more pushing up on inflation too: Governments are rushing to invest in energy security and defense. And the costs of key AI inputs like semiconductors are rising as capacity lags demand.

This is the backdrop facing the Federal Reserve and other major central banks as they meet this week.

3) Diversification mirage

All this doesn’t bode well for government bonds, which have struggled to offset equity declines throughout the Middle East conflict. This underscores what we call a "diversification mirage" in our Global Outlook. We think it’s a core feature of the post-pandemic world.

We prefer stocks over bonds as we see persistent inflation pressures keeping interest rates higher for longer. We also see thematic opportunities linked to AI.

____________

Supply disruptions emanating from the Middle East conflict have piled onto inflation pressures that were already bubbling under the surface. This week’s central bank run lays bare the bind policymakers face between reining in inflation and supporting growth and jobs. We think higher yields are here to stay - and that long-term government bonds are no longer effective diversifiers against equity declines. We stay overweight U.S. and EM equities on the rapid AI buildout.

Too hot for comfort

Note: The line for the U.S. shows core services excluding shelter PCE inflation. Inset shows inflation dynamics for the latest 12 months.

Markets projected U.S. rate cuts before the Iran war erupted - and ignored signs that inflation’s downward trend had already stalled as core services inflation remained stubbornly high. See the chart’s inset on the right. The reason: An aging population and immigration curbs make for a tight labor market. Add the AI-led capex boom and tariff-driven goods inflation, and it becomes clear why broader core inflation is running above central bank targets. Markets flip-flopped when the Middle East supply disruptions caused price spikes in energy and base chemicals. They are now pricing out U.S. rate cuts this year and expect the European Central Bank to hike instead of staying put. This shift reflects a recognition that inflation is running above pre-pandemic levels - a trend we see persisting for now.

The big picture is that we are in a world shaped by supply. The Middle East conflict has supercharged existing supply constraints and intensified mega forces - structural changes such as the energy transition, geopolitical fragmentation and AI disruption. Supply disruptions of energy, chemicals and other industrial materials are increasing inflation pressures, albeit with disparate effects across regions. Europe and parts of Asia are feeling the brunt given their dependence on imported energy. The U.S. is more shielded as a net energy exporter. The conflict is reinforcing the resolve of governments around the world to invest in energy security and defense, adding to towering debt loads and putting upward pressure on inflation.

A stark trade-off for central banks

At the same time, an accelerating AI buildout is creating outsized demand for energy, data centers and specialized labor. This is bumping into worsening capacity and political constraints that are increasing costs. Prices of key AI inputs such as semiconductors are rising as capacity struggles to keep pace with demand. We think AI’s productivity gains could quickly offset such “chipflation” and other price pressures - and push down inflation. But this hasn’t happened yet.

Such is the backdrop for the Fed and other major central banks as they meet this week. They face a stark trade-off between trying to bring down inflation or supporting economic growth and jobs. No change in policy rates is expected, and the key is to watch for signs whether policymakers are growing concerned about persistent inflation, even if they look through price pressures caused by Middle East supply disruptions.

We stay risk-on in this environment. We are overweight U.S. and EM equities as major AI firms are now showing they can monetize their tools. We stay underweight long-term government bonds. They struggled to offset equity declines throughout the Iran war. This underscores the “diversification mirage” outlined in the Q2 update to our 2026 Global Outlook: This is a structural feature of the post-pandemic environment as the term premium - the extra compensation investors demand for holding long-term bonds - rises on concerns over high debt loads. We prefer short-dated credit and Treasuries for quality income instead, and EM hard-currency debt as it leans toward commodity exporters benefiting from supply disruptions.

Our bottom line

We prefer stocks over bonds as we see inflation pressures keeping interest rates higher for longer. We also eye thematic opportunities across power and infrastructure on AI demand and a scramble for energy security.

Market backdrop

The S&P 500 notched a fresh record even as oil prices rose as shipping traffic in the Strait of Hormuz almost crawled to a halt. This shows the AI mega force is shaping up even stronger than we envisaged in our 2026 Global Outlook. Previous market skepticism over AI - that major players were spending heavily but not making money - is dissipating and turning into belief. AI adoption is rising and revenue growth is accelerating.

We focus on a packed week of central bank decisions. We expect the Fed, ECB, BoE and BoJ to hold rates steady - though the latter is more finely balanced. Even if the BoJ stays on hold, we expect policymakers to signal more hikes are on the table this year. We watch the ECB and BoE decisions to see if they will look through energy-driven inflation rather than respond to the risk of it spreading into wider price pressures.

This post originally appeared on BlackRock

data.title: Tesla: Why Q1 Results Were A Disappointment
data.description:
Overview

Having covered six of the Mag-7 in my previous article, I now move my focus to Tesla, Inc. (TSLA). Personally, I do not think there is anything magnificent about Tesla except the ridiculous P/E ratio it trades at despite the pathetic growth it continues to post. Whether it is some mysterious call-buying activity, retail investors believing the robotaxi/humanoid robot promises, the Elon Musk halo effect, or whatever else, it is fruitless to attempt to understand the reason for this monstrous P/E ratio.

In this article, I will first cover the Q1 earnings. After this, I’ll dive deeper into the automobile business, the energy business, and robotaxis. Tesla's Q1 numbers hide the underlying weakness of the business, which will likely become more visible to everyone in the coming quarters.

Q1 Financials: One-Offs Hide the Weakness

Tesla announced its Q1 financial results after the markets closed on 22nd April, mostly beating consensus estimates. The below image shows how Tesla’s financials fared against expectations and in comparison to Q1 2025.

I am slightly taken aback by the growth in revenues, considering that Q1 deliveries grew only 6% YoY and the energy business also experienced degrowth. Gross margin was also a major surprise. While these are all encouraging and bullish, it is important that we understand the drivers behind the results and reported beats.

Tesla earned an additional $0.9 billion in revenue due to favorable movement in foreign exchange rates. It also saved $0.2 billion in costs for the same reason. So, the net benefit from FX tailwinds was $1.1 billion, which is higher than the company’s operating profit, or EBIT, of $941 million. With the dollar index now flat YoY, it is unlikely that these factors will lift profitability in the coming quarter(s).

Another contributor to revenues is potentially SpaceX (SPACE) buying Tesla Cybertrucks. We know that SpaceX contributed $100 million to Tesla’s revenues in Q4 2025. It would not surprise me if that was also the case in Q1 2026.

Another contributor to Tesla’s profitability was some one-time or one-off benefits. Automobile segment gross profit improved due to "warranty true-downs around $230 million and some additional relief on tariffs." The energy business margins also benefited due to one-time benefits of $250 million tied to prior-quarter tariff recognitions. The below table shows Tesla’s gross profit and gross margins if adjusted for the one-time benefits.

Vertical Revenue COGS Gross Profit Margin (%) Adj. Gross Profit Adj. Margin (%) Q4 2025 Q1 2025
Auto 16,234 12,812 3,422 21.08% 3,192 19.66% 20.42% 16.2%
Energy 2,408 1,456 952 39.5% 702 29.15% 28.61% 28.75%

That’s another half a billion in profit that is unlikely to be earned in the next quarter in addition to the $1.1 billion that likely will not be earned in the coming quarter. Let’s not forget the revenue from the sale of credits is also on the decline. What these accounting anomalies do is distort the picture and hide the weakness in Tesla’s profitability.

A similar pattern is seen in the cash flow statement too. While Tesla's sales were down sequentially, the company's accounts payable grew further by $1.4 billion. Tesla's accounts payable have grown by $1.877 billion since the end of Q3 2025, in spite of largely flattish sales. These accounts payable will reverse at some point in the future, adding burden to the company's cash flow/liquidity position. Andre Thierig (Sr. Director of Manufacturing at Giga Berlin) announced that the company will increase Model Y output by 20% at the Gigafactory in Berlin. This could end up making things worse, as inventory has been draining cash flow for four of the last five quarters. Tesla's inventory has grown to 27 days of global supply from 10 days at the end of Q3 2025.

Tesla reported positive free cash flow for the quarter, but that was because CapEx was low at $2.5 billion compared to the company’s previous guidance of $20 billion, which was now revised upwards to $25 billion. As a result, the company expects to have negative free cash flow in the year. It remains to be seen whether this will be funded by debt (a burden on profitability and higher risk) or equity dilution or a mixture of both.

Business Segments:

Auto Business: Signs of Revival?

Whether retail investors accept it or not, the fact is that Tesla is an automobile company at the end of the day. Automobile revenues contributed to 73.3% of Tesla’s revenues and 72.3% of the company’s gross profit in 2025. In Q1 2026, auto revenues made up 72.5% of the company’s total revenues.

During Q1 2026, the company delivered 358,023 vehicles against consensus estimates of 365,000 to 370,000 vehicles. Deliveries declined 14% from the previous quarter but are up 6% compared to the same quarter, which is at least slightly encouraging as there was no decline, but the company needs a lot higher growth to justify the valuations.

To my surprise, the auto business was the bright spot in this quarter. For the company to have grown delivery volumes alongside some price hikes is undeniably a big positive. The newer, cheaper versions of the Model Y and the Model 3 appear to have been received well by the consumers. Higher oil prices created tailwinds for EV sales in Europe, and a weaker dollar (compared to Q1 2025) also helped create FX tailwinds from foreign sales, as the company would realize more dollars from the same number of foreign currency units.

Personally, I would want to see further acceleration in delivery volume growth and margin expansion before I become sure about whether this growth is a proper turnaround or a one-off quarter.

Energy: Weak Commentary in Q1

In the last couple of years, the automobile business failed to deliver strong growth, but the energy business has done a lot better. The below graph shows Tesla’s quarterly revenues from the energy business as well as the YoY growth.

However, concerns are emerging about whether this strong performance will continue. During the Q1 earnings call, CFO Vaibhav Taneja warned about increasing competition and the impact of tariffs. He said that the impact of tariffs is felt the most in the energy business, as most of the battery cells are imported from China. In my opinion, competition would not only hurt future growth potential but could also impact gross margins across the industry, and Tesla would not be shielded from it, in my opinion.

Since the energy business tends to be lumpy and less consistent throughout the years, I would yet again want to wait for a quarter or two before passing a clear judgement.

Robotaxi: Waymo Is What Bulls Think Tesla Is and Can Be

Tesla's unsupervised full self-driving, or the Tesla robotaxis, went from supposedly having no competition in the eyes of the bulls at one time to facing rising competition not just from Alphabet's (GOOG) Waymo but also from newer entrants such as Amazon's (AMZN) Zoox. Until not so long ago, the robotaxi was supposed to be the key contributor to the supposed value in TSLA that bears like me were said to be overlooking, even though I wrote articles with detailed calculations. Regardless, let’s dive deeper into what is happening on this front for Tesla.

Let me go through the positives first. Tesla began its robotaxi pilot in the city of Austin with a 20-square-mile coverage area, which has since grown to 245 square miles, for which it must get the credit it deserves. The percentage of unsupervised rides is 36% in the last 7 days and 25.7% in the last 30 days. What’s disappointing is that only 58 rides were done (or at least sampled) in the last week, out of which 21 were fully unsupervised.

However, even these cars are monitored remotely and are not fully autonomous. In October 2025, CEO Elon Musk told the All-In podcast that Tesla would have more than 500 autonomous vehicles in the Greater Austin Area by the end of the year. However, the actual number is under a tenth of that. As of the time of writing, out of 96 Tesla test vehicles in Austin, 51 have been inactive in the last 30 days. Out of 45, only 13 vehicles are being tested on a fully supervised basis. Additionally, the data shows that an unsupervised Tesla crashes once every 55,000 miles to 57,000 miles. This is anywhere between four and nine times more often than human drivers crash.

There is also a transparency gap when it comes to explanations for crashes. Waymo, Zoox, and others use the ‘narrative section’ to detail the cause of the crash, complex lane changes or intersections, and behavior of drivers in the other vehicle(s). This provides the regulatory body (NHTSA) and consumers with valuable information to understand the limitations of autonomous vehicles. Tesla, on the other hand, takes a very contrasting approach. The company always redacts its crash narrative section. While the company claims it is done to protect business secrets, in my opinion, it likely reduces the level of trust that the regulator and the public will have in Tesla’s unsupervised FSD systems.

The below image shows the fleet growth of Tesla’s robotaxis since it launched nine to ten months ago. Total active (seen at least once in the last 30 days) fleet is down from its peak of 234 on 8th March 2026 to 142 currently. The decline is in large part due to Bay Area operations, where the fleet has decreased massively from 189 on 8th March 2026 to 97 as of 23rd April 2026. The fleet in Austin, too, has nearly halved from its peak of 79 in late January 2026 to about 45 currently. These are not encouraging signs, especially when Waymo’s total fleet has doubled in the last year. Lastly, Elon Musk clarified that robotaxi revenues will grow to be materially significant only next year.

As for the competition, Waymo is already doing half a million rides every week. Elon Musk can ridicule and mock Waymo all he likes, but the ground reality is that they are years ahead of Waymo on the robotaxi. In fact, Waymo is what Tesla bulls think Tesla is. While the multiple sensor technology is more expensive, I still feel Waymo is more scalable than Tesla’s unsupervised FSD/robotaxi model. Waymo’s commitment and responsibility are limited to the sensors and the software, and so it can sell this package to almost anyone, after which the person can list their vehicle on a ride-hailing app or simply have it for personal use. Instead of dealing with individuals, Waymo can simply partner with other automobile manufacturers (Tesla’s competitors) and offer them the technology without having to worry about the manufacturing of the vehicles. I find it easier to envision a future where Waymo sensors and models will be on all cars, like the embedded Google Maps on automobile software systems.

However, Tesla is also responsible for manufacturing robotaxis, which lowers gross margins, and they need to crack the autonomous vehicle technology with far fewer detection devices (camera only, no RADAR/LIDAR). After that, they need to have sufficient control over the cost of the cameras and the regular bill of goods in manufacturing a car, which significantly lowers their margins and earning potential, in my opinion.

Conclusion

I would like to conclude by reminding investors that it is generally sensible to analyze earnings in some detail and not be moved by the headline/reported numbers. That is certainly helpful in analyzing Tesla's Q1 results, where a lot of one-offs hid the true weakness of the company.

I also discuss the outperformance of the automobile business, concerns around the energy business, and how investors might be over-optimistic around Tesla robotaxis.

data.title: MSTU: Amplified Exposure To A Leveraged Bitcoin Stock
data.description:
The T-REX 2X Long MSTR Daily Target ETF (MSTU) is a leveraged exchange-traded fund designed to provide investors with 2x the daily performance of Strategy Inc. (MSTR) shares. MSTR shares are generally viewed by the market as a leveraged Bitcoin investment, potentially adding exceptional risk when considering MSTU as a trading strategy. With a daily reset compounding performance when held longer than a single day, MSTU should only be used by sophisticated traders seeking to amplify their exposure to MSTR and, in turn, Bitcoin.

About T-REX 2X Long MSTR Daily Target ETF

MSTU was launched by Rex Shares on October 19, 2023, on the Cboe BZX Exchange as part of its leveraged single-stock ETF series. The strategy has a total expense ratio of 105 bps, a relatively modest fee when compared to peer leveraged MSTR ETFs on the market.

The fund exhibits substantial liquidity with $557 million in net assets, with an average of $205 million in share value changing hands on a daily basis. Liquidity should facilitate an active market for traders to enter and exit positions, though the fund has an average bid/ask spread of 0.22%, potentially adding costs to one’s performance.

MSTU was designed to provide traders with 200% daily exposure to MSTR shares, meaning that if MSTR shares appreciated by 1% in a single trading day, MSTU may appreciate by 2%. MSTU utilizes total return swap (TRS) derivatives in order to gain leveraged exposure to MSTR shares. TRSs have some intricacies that may influence the performance of the fund, particularly when held for longer than a single trading day.

TRSs are derivative contracts that provide the receiver exposure to the total returns of the underlying asset in exchange for a fixed rate. TRSs are settled on a daily basis, with performance resetting on the following trading day. This is reflected in the performance of MSTU, in which the ETF will reflect the daily performance with performance resetting on the following trading day. As a result of this feature, holding MSTU for longer than a single trading day can result in a compounding effect that may amplify gains or losses, particularly if the underlying stock moves in the same direction over multiple consecutive days.

The downside risk is that performance isn’t 1-for-1 in terms of rolling returns vs. compounded returns. For example, if an investor were to place $100 into MSTU shares and MSTR shares were to appreciate by 1%, the investor would gain 2% and close the day with $102. If shares were to decline by -1% on the following trading day, the investor’s position would decline to $99.96 rather than $100. Over time, this can be value-destructive if the investor remains positioned in MSTU shares, possibly resulting in significant loss in value.

If shares were to undergo multiple consecutive days of declines, the value lost may be unrecoverable given that the compounding performance is more heavily weighted to the downside risk.

It's also important to note that Strategy also uses financial leverage (debt) with regard to Bitcoin. This can be found on its balance sheet. It also issues preferred shares, which act as a soft leverage. This leverage is different from the leverage used by MTSU, which serves to amplify the effects of Strategy's price movement.

Investor Suitability

MSTU shares should only be used by seasoned traders seeking to amplify their daily exposure to MSTR shares. MSTU should not be considered as a long-term investment strategy given the significant risks associated with the structure of the fund and the amplified exposure to bitcoin. Given that MSTR shares are valued as a leveraged bitcoin strategy, MSTU shares can be inadvertently valued as a leveraged ETF of a leveraged bitcoin strategy. Compared to market peers, MSTU exhibits the most trading depth, making it a more optimal trading tool for this type of product.

Additional Risks Related To MSTU

MSTU employs 200% exposure to MSTR shares, exposing investors to substantial risks that should be considered prior to making a final investment decision. Trading MSTU shares can provide investors with significant exposure to the price of bitcoin, adding substantial risk to an investor’s position if the market were to take a risk-off tone. Holding MSTU for longer than a single trading day can result in compounded performance that may adversely impact one’s investment. Leveraged strategies expose investors to NAV decay over time given the structure of the funds, adding weight to the downside when considering all other risks.

Short-term trading may add substantial capital gains taxes on top of management fees, spread risk, and brokerage fees that should be considered when evaluating total expected returns of a trade.

You can review additional risks here and the SEC bulletin here.

Given the risky nature of this fund, traders should adhere to strict risk management protocol and sell discipline, as a bad trade can worsen in a short period of time due to the compounding effect of the fund.

Final Thoughts

MSTU is a 2x leveraged trading strategy designed to provide 200% the daily exposure to MSTR shares. Given that MSTR is viewed as a leveraged bitcoin strategy, trading MSTU can be considered an amplified tool for trading bitcoin. Given the risky nature of this ETF, traders should take significant precautions before trading this fund and set risk parameters to protect their initial investments from significant losses.

This article answers three questions about MSTU:

  1. What is MSTU's relationship to Strategy, Inc. and Bitcoin?
  2. What risks accompany using MSTU?
  3. What should investors consider when assessing MSTU's fit for their portfolio?

Editor's note: This article is intended to provide a general overview of the ETF for educational purposes only and, unlike other articles on Seeking Alpha, does not offer an investment opinion about the ETF.

data.title: UnitedHealth: Went Back Up Too Far, Too Fast (Downgrade)
data.description:
UnitedHealth: Back In Positive Zone For 2026

Health insurance stocks are finally having their moment again, outperforming the market hands down.

Just when everyone thought that health insurance was going down further, as always, when pessimism is taken too far south, the reversal is also just as compelling.

The derating attributed to the advanced Medicare rate notice that cratered the stock of UnitedHealth Group Incorporated (UNH) has more than fully reversed for the year.

UNH is now back on track to notch double-digit gains year-to-date, outperforming its closest peers.

UnitedHealth: Medicare Rate Changes Helped A Lot

Of course, we have to give due credence to the +2.48% upward revision in the Medicare rate revision finalized for 2027.

As the largest player in this market, it is no surprise that the market has given more benefit of the doubt to UNH since my last update.

Back in my previous UnitedHealth write-up, I had mentioned that 2026 was shaping up to be an incredibly challenging year.

One that’s filled with unpredictable volatility as investors debate whether the reset and inflection are really taking place.

However, with UNH’s valuation down well below its long-term average, it did seem like the market was baking in worse scenario plays that could emerge from the CMS decision on the rate notice.

Therefore, even though the +2.48% increase was nothing much to shout about, it was still better than nothing, right?

Definitely more productive than the flat rate that was formerly prognosticated, as worst-case scenarios haunted UNH investors earlier in 2026.

With the dust on the CMS settled, it really helped the stock settle into the period heading into its Q1 earnings in the third week of April.

Given how powerful the recovery has been for UnitedHealth, have the bulls finally cast out the ghosts that have been haunting the stock since it peaked in late 2024?

UnitedHealth: Second Half May Be Tougher Than The First

I think the key impediments relating to UNH aren’t so much about expecting a really dramatic turnaround that saw its EPS scaling up a nice secular uptrend before it fell apart in late 2024 or early 2025, right?

The fact that UNH continues to rejiggle its coverage portfolio by lowering its exposure to less profitable markets has contributed to the improved outcome.

After all, the reduction in the medical costs ratio was mainly attributed to “net reserve development, better mix, and enrollment dynamics in government programs.”

Is the work done yet? Of course not.

MCR at 83.9% marks an improvement from 84.8%, but the work isn’t done. The company clearly highlighted that Q1 was also helped favorably by “seasonal dynamics.”

More importantly, the tailwinds that we saw in Q1 aren’t likely to be fully replicated, as management anticipates further normalization of the growth curve through the second half.

In other words, while the stock has climbed up from its battering in Q1, the MCR readjustment is not even close to completion.

Which is why the medical rate upward revision really helped. However, the question again boils down to whether these are truly structural changes that we can expect moving forward.

UnitedHealth's Growth Phase May Be Long Over

I don’t wish to speculate. But health insurance is a policy time bomb, no?

Both for the policymakers and also the health insurers.

Insurers facing high MCRs now face extreme pressure to amend their benefits and coverage in line with the rate revisions.

UNH’s inability to continue on its previous growth path has demonstrated that what worked in the past is no longer sustainable.

Which is why the market has called into question whether the stock still deserves a premium above its long-term average.

Which probably also explains why UNH investors haven’t been able to reconcile these adjustments fully, as we don’t have a crystal ball into whether things could change after the midterms later this year.

In this case, UNH has made careful mention of its strategic approach at its Q1 earnings conference.

That appears to be unequivocal to me. I’m confident that the growth-focused posture that investors were more comfortable with previously has given way to portfolio discipline.

And that discipline entails prioritizing “margin recovery and product stability with a deliberate trade-off on membership growth, particularly in Medicare and commercial markets.”

However, like I mentioned before, health insurance and premiums are a dicey topic that could cast unfavorable optics on UNH and its peers.

And based on the enrollment dynamics prevalent in UNH’s management commentary, I’m fairly certain that we should see a more measured pace in topline growth, but still translated into favorable adjusted EPS growth.

Why? Firstly, the low base from 2025 definitely helps.

Then, the rate increases help tremendously with the valuation multiple rerating and also alleviate pressure on downward EPS revisions.

Accordingly, analysts have been marking up the EPS estimates, even though topline growth may slow further.

At this juncture, I think that’s the right move, to try and bring UnitedHealth’s operating margins back in line with the long-term average, as seen above.

Health insurance is a tough business (relatively low margins), but one that benefits the scaled player, such as UNH.

However, regulatory uncertainties have also cast a big gloom over future CMS rate revisions. On that, I don't think it's a straightforward case for investors, not anymore.

Buy Or Sell UNH Stock?

For those of you who didn’t manage to get on board UNH before the recent surge, I think it’s a good time to take a step back and reassess whether momentum investors could credibly mount a breakout of the $365 level.

UNH has rallied rapidly for the past four weeks, holding ground over the $265 zone (observe the vertical price print).

That is a very important milestone, because it corroborates my view that the July 2025 lows have held firmly.

That indicates that the market felt confident enough with UNH’s improved operating metrics translating into its P&L, which spurred analysts to mark up their estimates, as I explained earlier.

However, the market has also reacted faster than I thought to these developments.

At this point, UNH’s forward P/E of 18.8x has rallied back to the 10-year average of 18.9x. So that disconnect has clearly been digested.

Next, UNH is also retesting the key resistance level at the $365 level that has actually been denying further upside since Q3 of 2025.

I think for any further upward print to materialize fruitfully, this level must be taken with high conviction. No clear indications yet.

Until then, we should simply assume that UNH’s second half will be easier than the first, because the market appears to have run ahead of the fundamentals.

Especially since management did caution that some of the favorable seasonality could turn down from Q2.

So, with the market already pricing in a noticeably stronger second half, I believe the optimism is reflected.

Rating: Downgrade to Hold.

data.title: AMZA, Over 8% Distribution On The Hottest Segment Right Now: MLP Midstream
data.description:
Among the ETFs tied to the MLP midstream segment, there's one with distributions above 8% that's capturing more and more attention: the InfraCap MLP ETF (AMZA). Over the last six months this sector has benefited from the conflict in Iran. And here's a problem: judging by the movements of other markets, a normalization of energy prices seems to be coming, a positive element for the economic system but potentially bringing "negativity" to the stock market segments tied to energy.

The question I ask myself is: does this invalidate the underlying path of AMZA and the MLP midstream segment? While on one hand I think this could amplify volatility, I think it does not invalidate the long-term dimension in which this segment, especially thanks to AI demand, has positioned itself. But before getting into the heart of the matter, let's look at AMZA more closely.

What is AMZA?

It is an actively managed ETF that does not replicate an index. The fund has over 11 years of history and an official total Expense Ratio (February 2026 prospectus) of 1.72% (to which a 30-Day Median Bid/Ask spread of 0.60% is added, a high spread for a $444M ETF)

The fund uses as its benchmark the Alerian MLP Infrastructure Index: a composite of MLP energy infrastructure, built with a float-adjusted capitalization-weighted methodology, total return basis.

How is it built?

It is an actively managed fund with 100% focus on US energy infrastructure, meaning focus on MLP midstream: gathering, transportation, processing, and storage of natural gas, NGL, crude oil, and refined products. Management does security selection and weights based on fundamental analysis at the individual security level and technical factors, not on market capitalization, with a logic of overweighting higher quality names and tactical adjustments based on relative valuation and momentum. It then introduces a maximum limit per single issuer: 15% of total assets (prospectus). Which results in a high portfolio turnover, where in the fiscal year ended 10/31/2025 it was 237% of the average portfolio value: meaning the portfolio is turned over on average 2.37 times a year.

The result

Declared typical portfolio: 25-35 MLPs, but the top 10 holdings are still 97% of the equity exposure today, so the fund is de facto a concentrated basket of 6-10 names with leverage.

Its strength? The dividend yield. It has a 30-Day SEC Yield (as of 03/31/2026) of 6.69% and a Distribution Rate (as of 04/23/2026) of 8.95%. How does it get there? The fund also writes covered calls to generate additional income and reduce stated volatility. It does so with authorized leverage of up to 33%.

Peer comparison

And it finds a comparably interesting comparison with these other ETFs also vertical on the MLP midstream segment.

Where I personally reworked the peer information, aggregating it into this summary table:

  • Clearly AMZA is the most expensive on the list, with a difference of 137 bps compared to the cheapest, and likewise AMZA is also the smallest in the group.
  • But it is also the highest in terms of distributions, with a distribution ratio we've seen of 8.95%, not negligible, naturally due to a more articulated structure and active management.
  • At the taxation level, together with AMPL, it has C-corp, full corporate taxation (perhaps the most structurally penalized).

If I had to narrow the field to direct peers, I'd say AMLP is the C-Corp benchmark closest to AMZA. And in terms of total return, in this sense AMZA, despite costing double, has managed to deliver more competitive returns. The same cannot be said compared to instruments like MLPX and ENFR, which, however, have incomparable yields (over 450 bps of spread), and both have a different structure, with fewer MLPs to maintain no K-1 and no corporate taxes.

The market context

The current composition of AMZA is dominated by crude oil and NGL (Energy Transfer, Plains, Sunoco, MPLX = over 55% of the fund).

Two arguments that today deserve particular attention considering the conflict in Iran: with the outbreak of the conflict in February, AMZA and peers had very competitive returns.

A positive trend destined to fade? Potentially to scale back. The ceasefire brought this potential to light. The market sold oil and natural gas at the highs of the bull cycle: expected inflation scaled back. The 1Y Break Even Inflation (BEI) dropped back below 3%, and the 5Y and 10Y remain below 2.5%; in essence, the market is pricing in a stabilization of energy prices. A trend, however, that in my opinion is not destined to fade.

Why not fade? My opinion

Because it's precisely conflicts like these that bring to light the importance of the energy sector and the MLP midstream segment. Ask yourself: why is there so much tension behind energy prices? Because they've become a complex resource: the demand for natural gas from AI data centers (xAI Colossus 2 at $20B, Fermi America) is identified by management itself as the main structural catalyst for the midstream sector in the coming years. In this context, the directly identified beneficiaries: The Williams Companies (WMB), Kinder Morgan (KMI), TC Energy (TRP), and DT Midstream (DTM), all gas-focused names, inside AMZA. All elements that keep the underlying trend of this sector positively linear. So, while I expect the normalization of energy prices to potentially bring short-term negative volatility, I think it does not invalidate the expected long-term returns of the sector. Which, in my opinion, justifies a HOLD rating.

Risk

And how much could this short-term volatility amount to? In my opinion, it's fair to know that historical volatility is close to 20%, significantly above average. There have been quarters in which the fund recorded drops of as much as 74%, as in Q1 2020.

Conclusion

I think the right rating for AMZA is HOLD. This is because the recent geopolitical developments and the interpretation the market is giving to President Trump's ceasefire seem to be pointing toward a progressive normalization of energy prices. An element that in the short term can bring volatility. But the importance of energy distribution remains an increasingly central theme: in support, there are factors that justify an underlying bullish view on AMZA.

data.title: Record U.S. Shipbuilding Request To Benefit Nuclear Names
data.description:
By Steffan Szumowski

The U.S. Navy’s FY2027 shipbuilding request, released earlier this month, calls for $65.8 billion in funding, marking the highest single-year shipbuilding ask in decades. Over one-third of that total, or more than $25 billion, targets nuclear-powered platforms. This includes advance procurement and construction dollars for ballistic missile submarines and attack submarines, as well as refueling and overhaul work on the carrier fleet.

Nuclear propulsion gives U.S. submarines and carriers unmatched endurance, stealth, and operational flexibility. Each platform relies on specialized fuel, reactor components, pumps, valves, heat exchangers, and instrumentation. The funding surge directly supports the Naval Nuclear Propulsion Program; consequently, it creates steady, long-term revenue streams for companies already active across the nuclear value chain.

Due to their extensive history of securing government contracts, several companies within the VettaFi Nuclear Renaissance Index (NUKZX) are well-positioned to capitalize on the increase in funding for nuclear programs. NUKZX is the underlying index for the Range Nuclear Renaissance Index ETF (NUKZ).

U.S. Companies Positioned for Naval Nuclear Tailwinds

BWX Technologies (BWXT) serves as the primary manufacturer of naval nuclear fuel and reactor components for the U.S. submarine and carrier fleet. The company has delivered fuel and hardware for every class of nuclear-powered vessel in the Navy inventory.

In parallel with its naval propulsion work, BWXT is advancing critical government-side fuel capabilities. Under a $1.5 billion contract with the National Nuclear Security Administration (NNSA), BWXT is reestablishing domestic uranium enrichment capacity. More specifically, this project will initially demonstrate low-enriched uranium production before transitioning to highly enriched uranium for naval propulsion needs.

Curtiss-Wright Corp. (CW) supplies mission-critical pumps, valves, generators, and instrumentation that operate at the heart of naval nuclear propulsion plants. The company has supported every U.S. Navy nuclear submarine and aircraft carrier program for decades. Its components are integral to reactor coolant systems and auxiliary power, making CW a key enabler of the submarine and carrier build-out included in the budget request.

Graham Corporation (GHM), through its specialized fluid and heat transfer technologies, provides steam plant equipment tailored for nuclear-powered surface ships and submarines. These systems manage steam and cooling demands within the propulsion plants of select submarines and carriers. Follow-on orders for these programs have already delivered multi-year visibility to revenues. The new shipbuilding dollars are expected to sustain and expand that pipeline.

Lockheed Martin (LMT) applies its extensive nuclear engineering expertise to both defense and space programs. The company has an extensive history in naval nuclear program support, and is a key partner with NASA and the Defense Advanced Research Projects Agency on advanced space nuclear technologies, including nuclear thermal propulsion. This work leverages Lockheed’s proven capabilities in high-reliability nuclear systems and underscores the expanding role of nuclear technology beyond terrestrial power and naval propulsion.

UK Parallel Reinforces Global Naval-to-Civilian Synergies

In the United Kingdom, Rolls-Royce (RYCEY, RYCEF, RR.LN) has long served as the design authority for the Royal Navy’s nuclear propulsion plants (read more). The company’s pressurized water reactor technology powers the UK submarine fleet and provides the foundational expertise for its small modular reactor (SMR) program.

On April 13, 2026, Rolls-Royce SMR signed a landmark contract with Great British Energy - Nuclear to advance the UK’s first SMR fleet. This builds directly on decades of naval nuclear experience, accelerating the transition of proven propulsion technology into commercial power applications. The UK example illustrates how naval programs can seed and de-risk civilian nuclear deployment, creating additional opportunities for supply chain partners with dual-use capabilities.

Broader Benefits for NUKZX Investors

The Navy’s shipbuilding request supports both immediate procurement and long-lead industrial investments that benefit the same ecosystem of manufacturers, fuel providers, and service firms already positioned for commercial reactor growth. Naval work offers high barriers to entry, multi-decade contract visibility, and revenue streams that are largely insulated from commercial power-market cycles.

BWXT, CW, GHM, LMT, and RR.LN are all constituents of NUKZX. NUKZX includes companies across the nuclear value chain, from fuel and components to engineering, construction, and utilities.

In comparison to similarly themed ETFs, NUKZ exhibits significantly lower volatility due to the inclusion of companies in the industrial and utility sectors. Over the trailing 3-month and 12-month periods, NUKZ has recorded annualized volatility of 27% and 26%, respectively. A competing ETF with more uranium mining exposure saw annualized volatility of 46% and 42% for the same respective periods.

Disclosure: © VettaFi LLC 2026. All rights reserved. This material has been prepared and/or issued by VettaFi LLC ("VettaFi") and/or one of its consultants or affiliates. It is provided as general information only and should not be taken as investment advice. Employees of VettaFi are prohibited from owning individual MLPs. For more information on VettaFi, visit www.vettafi.com

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Amazon Q1: $200B In FY26 CapEx For A $15B Run-Rate Story
data.description:
Over the last 4 weeks, Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN) has outperformed the rest of the hyperscalers to the point that the stock has now re-rated at 34x next year's earnings.

That’s a rich multiple for a company with cloud business growth below Google's, which is trading at just 29x. No, I no longer buy the "it's a different revenue baseline" argument.

On top of that, the CEO disclosed in the latest letter to shareholders the AWS AI revenue run rate: $15B.

Let me be clear: to me, $15 in ARR doesn't justify a $200B CapEx spend for the year. Therefore, I want to see a significant upgrade to this figure throughout the year. This will be the key metric that I'll be monitoring this year.

On the AWS front, I want the first quarter results due on April 29th to crush the Street’s consensus. For reference, AWS grew 24% YOY in Q4, and the consensus now sits at around $36.8 billion for Q1, implying about 25.5% growth.

In comparison, the Street expects Google Cloud to grow 50% YOY in Q1 to $18.4B. Yes, it's half the revenue base, but that's no longer a valid excuse in a market where most tech investors are chasing growth.

Heading into the print, I prefer to remain on the sidelines. In my view, the print needs to prove that AWS growth, margins, and CapEx discipline can all hold together. Below, I explain why.

AWS: Is The CapEx Spending Warranted?

There is no doubt where the main focus will be in the upcoming earnings release: AWS growth.

Back in Q4, AWS grew 24% YOY to $35.6 billion. Interestingly, even operating income was up 17% YOY, showing that depreciation is not meaningfully pressuring the bottom line of this segment (yet).

I want to see a continuation of this trend. Specifically, I want to see AWS revenue coming in above the Street's consensus of $36.8B. That estimate would represent 25.4% YOY growth, which is relatively ok if one looks at the chart below, but I want to see more.

In Jassy's 2025 letter to shareholders, the CEO noted a list of "truths that are hard to debate." Among them, he disclosed for the first time ever that AWS AI's annualized revenue run rate was above $15 billion in Q1 2026. Here is the excerpt (emphasis added):

Three years into this AI wave, AWS’s AI revenue run rate is over $15 billion in Q1 2026 (nearly 260 times larger than AWS at that same point)—and ascending rapidly.

For reference, that AWS AI revenue run rate represents roughly 10% of Amazon Web Services' $142 billion revenue run rate. So, AI services are still a relatively small contribution to the AWS business.

Interestingly enough, the CEO also provided an update on the chip business, including Trainium, Graviton, and Nitro, which had an annual revenue run rate above $20 billion. To put that figure into perspective, back in Q4, the Trainium plus Graviton had an annual revenue run rate above $10 billion and was growing triple digits YOY.

On the AI chip roadmap, the current generation (Trainium3) is already running production workloads, and the company expects nearly all Trainium3 supply to be committed by mid-2026. As for Trainium4, this new generation of custom silicon is expected in 2027. Why are these chips so important?

Well, it turns out that they can save "tens of billions" (take it from CEO Jassy, not me) of dollars of capex per year at scale and provide a several hundred-basis-point AWS operating margin advantage versus simply relying on external chips for inference.

I love these ARR disclosures (AWS AI revenue and custom silicon) because analysts are likely to follow up in the remaining quarters on whether there are any revisions. Therefore, I'd keep a close eye on those two ARR metrics going forward.

The High-Margin Advertising Segment

Back in Q4, advertising services revenue was $21.3B, up 23% and 22% excluding FX. For the upcoming earnings release, the Street is projecting $16.84B in ad revenue, representing a growth rate of 21% YOY.

I see a risk here. The 21% that the Street expects would represent a step down from the growth seen in the last 3 quarters.

That outperformance was mainly driven by two forces: 3P sellers advertising on Amazon.com and the ad revenue from Prime Video. On Prime Video, the company disclosed in November last year 315M+ global monthly ad-supported viewers, up from the earlier 200M figure reported in April 2024.

On the 3P sellers, I see a direct relationship between 3P services revenue and ad revenue. Just take a look at the chart below, representing the 3P seller services revenue change, and compare it to the one above.

The more 3P sellers, the more bidding pressure on sponsored ad products on Amazon.com. That said, the only tailwind that I see this quarter for 3P seller revenue growth is an easy comp vs. Q1 2025, where the growth rate of this segment was only 6%.

Risks

Let's start with margins first. It seems that the analyst focus heading into Q1 is margin dispersion, especially for AWS. From what I'm reading, the Q1 AWS margin consensus has now moved down to 35.7% from 37.7% expected in October, with estimates ranging from 30.9% to 40.0%.

That dispersion is not great news heading into the print.

Another factor to consider is the CapEx. The company expects $200B in FY26 capital expenditures, mainly driven by AI, chips, robotics, and low-earth-orbit satellites.

The concern here is whether $200B in CapEx burn this year justifies the $15B AWS AI Services run rate. Right now, to me, it doesn't. Therefore, any upgrade in the $200B CapEx guide could lead to a similar reaction post earnings as back in Q4 2025, when the stock was down mid-single digits after the print.

As I said in my earnings previews for the other hyperscalers, I don't want to see any CapEx upgrades this year.

Valuation

On the valuation front, I see an expensive forward P/E multiple when compared to the rest of the hyperscalers.

The stock is trading at 34x next year's earnings. In comparison, the second most expensive hyperscaler, Alphabet (GOOG), is trading at 29x.

Does this make any sense to you, knowing that Alphabet is growing at a faster pace and Google Cloud is seeing a higher growth rate than AWS? To me, it doesn't, which is the reason I am now lowering my rating heading into the print.

Looking Ahead

Heading into the Q1 print, I will closely monitor whether AWS can meet or beat the Q1 consensus of roughly $36.8B, which implies about 25.5% YOY growth. Overall, I think the company needs to obliterate that estimate to justify the $200B CapEx plan this year.

In fact, I no longer buy the justification that AWS growth is slower than Google Cloud's because of a higher revenue base. With AI services revenue at only 10% of AWS’s run rate, I think there is plenty of room for improvement.

From a price action perspective, the stock is basically at new highs.

Whether the stock re-rates higher or not will largely depend on the AWS segment results in Q1 and the guidance for Q2 2026.

On the guidance, I think the focus will largely be on operating income.

As seen above, last quarter, there was a jump in operating income. For Q1, management guided a very wide range, between $16.5B and $21.5B. Here is an interesting remark from the Q4 earnings filing:

This guidance includes approximately $1 billion of higher year-over-year Amazon Leo costs as we scale in 2026, as well as investment in quick commerce and even sharper prices in our international stores business.

I didn't read the word depreciation above. That's a risk to consider, especially for the guide for Q2.

Overall, I prefer to remain on the sidelines heading into the print. That said, I see a spike in AWS growth beyond consensus, and while CapEx remains the same, I may reconsider my stance for the second quarter.

data.title: EUR/USD: Cautiously Bullish Above 1.1700 Ahead Of FOMC And ECB
data.description:
By Zain Vawda

EUR/USD (EUR:USD) finds itself grinding on a quiet Monday ahead of what is a busy week from a data and monetary policy perspective.

EUR/USD gapped lower after the weekend but has since recovered the weekend losses and is trading marginally higher on the day. This sort of price action could continue in the early part of the week, with volatility likely to peak on Wednesday and Thursday when we have the FOMC and ECB meetings.

Of course, any deal between the US and Iran in the interim could also shake up volatility and could potentially lead to USD weakness, as the safe haven bets may begin to unwind. Keep that in mind while reading the technical setups and opportunities that may materialize below.

H4 Chart Analysis: The Structural Shift

On the H4 time frame, the narrative has shifted from bearish to cautiously bullish. The most notable development is the price action reclaiming the 1.1700 psychological handle, which had previously acted as a stubborn ceiling.

The pair has successfully pushed above the 100, and 200-period Simple Moving Averages (MAs). Currently, price is rejecting the 50-day MA, opening up a potential retest of the 100-day MA at 17047. A sustained hold above the 1.1700-1.1710 support zone keeps the bullish bias firmly intact.

The RSI (Relative Strength Index) is currently hovering around 54, suggesting there is ample "runway" left before the pair reaches overbought territory.

H1 Chart Analysis: Consolidation Before the Next Leg?

Zooming into the H1 chart, we see a more granular view of the recent rally. The pair hit a local peak near 1.1754 (just shy of the 1.1769 resistance level) before edging lower.

Currently, the H1 price has rejected the 200-MA (blue line) as dynamic support. This is a critical junction; if the H1 can print a bullish engulfing candle or a long-wick rejection near this 1.1716-1.1700 area, it would signal that buyers are ready to defend the intraday trend.

The RSI on this time frame has cooled off from overbought levels, effectively "resetting" for a potential move higher with some leeway for a drop toward 50 first.

M15 Analysis and Scenarios: Tactical Planning for Upcoming Sessions

The Bullish Scenario: For the bulls to maintain control, they need to defend the 1.1710-1.1700 area. An ideal entry for a long position would be a successful retest of the M15 200-MA (blue line), followed by a break back above 1.1750.

  • Target 1: 1.1769 (recent high/horizontal resistance)
  • Target 2: 1.1800 (psychological level)
  • Invalidation: A 15-minute candle close below 1.1700

The Bearish Scenario: The bears need to see a breakdown of the current consolidation. If the price fails to hold the 1.1700 level, we could see a "stop-run" back toward the primary support zone.

  • Trigger: A break below 1.1700 would signal a deeper correction
  • Target 1: 1.1685 (value area high)
  • Target 2: 1.1643 April 8 swing low
  • Confirmation: Watch for the RSI on the M15 to drop below 50 to confirm momentum shift and look for an entry

Key Levels to Watch:

  • Resistance: 1.1769, 1.1800, 1.1867.
  • Support: 1.1700, 1.1680, 1.1643

The overall technical posture for EUR/USD remains tilted to the upside as long as the pair remains above the 1.1700 pivot zone. Traders should keep a close eye on the US Dollar Index (DXY) for broader directional cues, but from a pure price action standpoint, the bulls appear to be in the driver's seat heading into the next session.

Original Post

data.title: Expect Lennox International To Underperform The Market Moving Forward (Downgrade)
data.description:
Back in early October of last year, I warned investors that Lennox International Inc. (LII) was getting close to a downgrade. Even though revenue and profit growth had been strong, pushing the stock up more than the market for a few years in a row, and even though there was the opportunity for additional growth through acquisitions that the business had made, the fact of the matter is that the stock looked incredibly pricey. Still, I could not bring myself to downgrade it just yet. But since then, we have seen the stock drop about 10.5%. This compares to the 5% increase that the S&P 500 saw over the same window of time. Some of this drop could be the result of allegations of coordinated behavior with other players in this space. But certainly a contributor to the decline would be weakening financial performance. The most recent data that management has come out with, which covers through the final quarter of the 2025 fiscal year, saw revenue and profitability both drop. When you add on top of this management's own expectations for the next few years, it really is difficult to get behind. At the end of the day, I have no choice but to downgrade it to a "Sell."

This does not mean that my opinion can't or won't change as new data comes in. In fact, before the market opens on April 29, management will be announcing financial results for the first quarter of the company's 2026 fiscal year. Revenue is expected to be virtually flat, though profitability is forecast to fall. This further reinforces my view that a more cautious approach to the company is justified. But if the firm can come out with some really positive results, my opinion on the matter could certainly change.

Time To Cool Down

As I mentioned at the start of this article, the newest data that we currently have covers through the final quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. For the most part, I would say that this was not a particularly great quarter. Revenue, for starters, dropped to $1.20 billion. That happened to be down 11.2% compared to the $1.35 billion that the business reported a year earlier. As revenue declined, profitability also fell. Net income contracted from $201.9 million to $142.5 million. It is true that operating cash flow expanded from $332.4 million to $405.9 million. But if we adjust for changes in working capital, we get a drop from $244.2 million to $205.4 million. Finally, EBITDA for the company worsened from $280.6 million to $269.1 million.

In the chart above, you can see financial results for 2025 in their entirety compared to 2024. Once again, revenue, profits, and cash flows all dropped. However, there were a couple of bright spots when it came to cash flow. Adjusted operating cash flow and EBITDA, as an example, both increased year over year. So if anything, the final quarter of 2025 indicates an acceleration of the weakening that we saw earlier in the year.

The pain that the company exhibited specifically involved its Home Comfort Solutions segment. This is the part of the company that is responsible for dealing with furnaces, air conditioners, heat pumps, indoor air quality equipment, and more. Year over year, revenue plummeted from $887.4 million to $699.8 million. And for the year in its entirety, revenue contracted from $3.58 billion to $3.34 billion. Management mentioned that this drop was because of a decline in volumes. Even though for the year as a whole the company benefited to the tune of 10% from higher prices and a favorable change in product mix, volumes contracted 17%. Weakness in the residential new construction market for housing, which I have detailed in other articles, has been a pain point for the company. It doesn't help that suppliers are also continuing to destock inventory because of end market weakness on both the residential and commercial sides.

By comparison, the Building Climate Solutions segment has performed much better. For those not familiar, this part of the company provides unitary heating and air conditioning equipment, controls, installation and service of commercial heating and cooling equipment, variable refrigerant flow commercial products, and a wide array of other offerings to its customers. Year over year for the quarter, revenue jumped from $457.6 million to $495.2 million. And for the year in its entirety, the $1.85 billion generated by the segment exceeded the $1.76 billion reported the year prior. Management mentioned that the firm benefited for the year from higher prices and a change in product mix that collectively pushed revenue up. But on top of this, it also saw a 2% bump associated with its Duro Dyne acquisition. If only sales volumes had not fallen by 5%, revenue would have been even higher.

When it comes to the 2026 fiscal year, management seems quite optimistic. They expect sales to grow by between 6% and 7%. And, based on their own guidance, operating cash flow should be around $1.05 billion. If we project out other profitability metrics accordingly, we would be looking at net profits of about $837.5 million and EBITDA of $1.25 billion. But that's not all. The company actually has significant plans over the next few years. They anticipate that, from 2026 through 2030, industry unit growth should be around 3% per annum. This alone should pave the way for additional expansion. However, the company is also pushing for other means of expanding.

By the year 2030, revenue should grow to between $6.5 billion and $7.5 billion. Only $550 million of that should come from market growth. However, continued investments in certain growth opportunities should push sales higher by another $500 million. Plus, we are looking at a $575 million benefit from higher prices. On top of this, the firm is targeting $175 million dedicated toward acquisitions. If we also consider their current margin expansion initiatives, which include the benefit of price hikes, we would be looking at a profit margin expansion such that adjusted profit margins should grow from 20.4% last year to between 22% and 23% this year. We scale up other profitability metrics similarly; this would mean net income of about $1.20 billion, adjusted operating cash flow of $1.44 billion, and EBITDA of $1.72 billion.

Using these estimates, we can see in the chart above how the company is priced. As much as I wish I could say that these numbers excited me, they really don't. The stock is anything but cheap. In fact, the business seems to be more in the fair value range, as I would describe it. Even if management can achieve the targets that it is pushing for when it comes to the year 2030, if we assume that it should continue to trade at its existing multiples, this would translate to an annualized upside of between 8.7% and 8.9%. That's not bad, but it's not great when the market averages between 10% and 11% per year. Seeing as how I define a "Sell" candidate as one that is likely to underperform the market for the foreseeable future, Lennox International certainly fits that description.

Another problem here is that, relative to other similar companies, the stock is quite expensive. In the chart below, you can see precisely what I mean here. On a price-to-earnings basis, three of the five companies that I stacked it up against are cheaper than it is. But when it comes to both the price-to-operating cash flow approach and the EV-to-EBITDA approach, it is the most expensive of the group.

Even though I am taking a slightly negative stance on the business, my opinion could change as new data rolls in. Before the market opens on April 29, management will be announcing financial results for the first quarter of the company's 2026 fiscal year. The current expectation is for revenue to remain virtually flat at $1.07 billion. But earnings per share should decline from $3.37 to $3.11. That would take net profits down from $120.3 million to $109.2 million. Analysts, unfortunately, do not provide estimates when it comes to cash flow. But in the table below, you can see how the company performed when it comes to these metrics for the first quarter of the 2025 fiscal year. In all likelihood, weaker revenue and net income will translate to weaker cash flows as well.

Takeaway

The way I see things, Lennox International is an interesting business, but not one that is particularly compelling to me. In addition to some concerns on the legal front, the company is also priced at levels that don't instill much confidence. I wish that the stock was cheaper, but alas, it is not. And even if management achieves their targets for the next few years, I don't see it performing at or above what the market should enjoy. That's why I am downgrading it to a "Sell" at this time.

data.title: TSPY Vs. QDTE: Weighing 2 Ultra-High‑Yield Daily Options Income ETFs
data.description:
Intro

In today’s ever‑evolving ETF landscape, innovative new funds are constantly being launched as they try to earn their place under the sun and secure a spot in investors’ portfolios. One segment that has grown rapidly in recent years is ETFs that generate elevated income through options strategies. These funds can be categorized in several different ways, but one of the newest developments has emerged alongside a new type of option — or rather, traditional options with newly added expiration cycles, commonly known as zero‑day (0DTE) options.

To further demystify these 0DTEs: Major and most actively traded indices — such as the S&P 500 (SP500), Nasdaq‑100 (NDX), and Russell 2000 (RTY) — along with the ETFs built on them (SPY, QQQ, and IWM) now have options expiring every single trading day. In a sense, 0DTE options are new as a term, but at the same time, they aren’t in substance: every stock or ETF option becomes a 0DTE option on its final trading day and can therefore be considered a zero‑day option. For example, many actively traded stocks and ETFs have weekly options that expire every Friday, which means that once a week, there are hundreds of contracts that effectively qualify as 0DTEs.

The essential “birth” of modern‑day so‑called 0DTE options happened in 2022, when the CBOE added Tuesday and Thursday expirations to the most actively traded index products and their ETF equivalents. These products already had Monday, Wednesday, and Friday expirations, so adding two more days created a full five‑day expiration cycle.

These 0DTEs have been receiving wider public attention thanks to their rapid rise in popularity among traders. It’s now estimated that roughly half, or even a bit more, of the daily options volume in major indices and ETFs comes from trading these same-day expiring contracts.

Why Should Investors Consider 0DTE Options Using Income-Oriented ETFs

Most income‑oriented funds that use options deliver higher yields than traditional high‑yield products because their payouts are generated and boosted through various option‑selling strategies — and in that sense, 0DTE‑based funds serve the same purpose. There are, however, two key differences. The first is the duration of the options they use: 0DTE funds rely exclusively on intraday, same‑day‑expiring contracts. The second difference is linked to the first — this structure gives them a somewhat different risk profile.

Relying on intraday options exposes them to overnight risk in a different way than funds using longer‑dated contracts. In principle, this is the same overnight risk faced by their underlying indexes, which are exposed to price moves between regular trading sessions. Funds that use longer‑dated options stay exposed to them continuously, which usually caps their upside potential, but they also tend to offer better downside protection. A 0DTE fund, by contrast, ends each trading day with no short option positions, meaning it carries the same overnight risk as the underlying index itself: uncapped and unprotected, with no option hedges in place.

The most popular and actively traded 0DTE ETF, Roundhill Innov-100 0DTE Covered Call Strat ETF (QDTE) describes its mechanism of operation as follows:

QDTE seeks to provide overnight exposure to the Innovation-100 Index and generate income each morning by selling out-of-the-money 0DTE calls on the Index.

Another actively traded 0DTE ETF is TappAlpha SPY Growth & Daily Income ETF (TPSY). I’ll take a closer look at both and compare them to see which might offer a more attractive profile for investors interested in these types of funds.

Comparing QDTE and TPSY with Other Options‑Based Income Funds

I believe it’s great to begin with comparing 0DTE-based funds with other option-based, income-focused funds to see which group might deliver better returns. For reference, I looked at Global X NASDAQ 100 Covered Call ETF (QYLD) and NEOS S&P 500 High Income ETF (SPYI). Both QDTE and QYLD track the Nasdaq-100 index, while TSPY and SPYI are tied to the S&P 500.

In a one-year comparison, 0DTEs are clear winners, with QDTE outperforming QYLD by impressive 19.01% among Nasdaq-100 based funds. Meanwhile, TSPY edges out SPYI in the S&P 500 comparison by a narrower margin, but still by 4.19%:

Let’s take a look at the longer-term charts too. Since the last significant market pullback was in April of last year, I think it’s important to include that period in the comparison as well. We still can’t go beyond August 14, 2024, when the newest fund, TPSY, was launched.

The following chart shows that QDTE holds the top spot in both absolute and Nasdaq-100 index categories with a 39.44% total return over the 1 year and 8 months comparison, outperforming peer QYLD’s 27.42% by 12.02%. Taking second place is SPYI with a 29.45% return, edging out TSPY by just 0.58% and beating QYLD by 2.03%:

While QDTE clearly outperforms its peers, TPSY’s results are closely aligned with other comparables.

I think it’s appropriate to compare QDTE and TPSY to their benchmarks as well, so I used SPY and QQQ for comparison, as these are based on the S&P 500 and Nasdaq-100 indices.

Using the same maximum 1 year and 8 month chart for assessment, we can see that QDTE trails QQQ by 5.38% and TPSY trails SPY by 5.19%:

To sum up, I believe it’s fair to say that although QDTE and TPSY don’t outperform their underlying indexes, they come quite close, and when factoring in the compounding effect of reinvesting payouts, the gap narrows even further. So, in my view, if an investor prefers frequent, high payouts along with exposure to major indices, these funds are both great choices.

Let’s take a closer look at each fund in brief.

The TappAlpha SPY Growth & Daily Income ETF -TPSY

TappAlpha, a fund family managed by TappFinance, is a relatively new fund manager founded in 2023 and based in Seattle, Washington, that promotes itself as follows:

Our mission is to make advanced investing simple, actionable, and transparent. We demystify sophisticated investment strategies, providing you with easy-to-use tools to access Wall Street-caliber opportunities for growth and income.

They manage four funds, with TPSY being the largest in terms of both AUM and daily trading volume.

They present the TPSY edges:

The fund’s investment strategy involves permanently holding the SPY ETF and, at the start of each trading day, selling out-of-the-money same-day options for SPY, SPX, or XSP that are 0 to 5% out of the money at the time of purchase. It is an actively managed fund, where daily tactical decisions are based on the fund manager's market outlook.

Innovation-100 0DTE Covered Call Strategy ETF -QDTE

They Roundhill fund family introduce themselves as follows:

Founded in 2018, Roundhill Investments is an SEC-registered investment advisor focused on innovative exchange-traded funds. Roundhill’s suite of ETFs offers unique and differentiated exposures across thematic equity, options income, and trading vehicles. Roundhill offers a depth of ETF knowledge and experience, as the team has collectively launched more than 100+ ETFs including several first-to-market products.

The fund explains how their fund benefits from the underlying 0DTE strategy:

What are the potential benefits of selling options at the market open?

By selling 0DTE options at market open, QDTE potentially allows for investors to capture the full return of the overnight session. Unlike strategies which may hold short option positions overnight, QDTE is “uncapped” from market close until a new short call position is entered the following session.

Description of their option selling process from Prospectus:

At market open, or shortly thereafter, on every business day, the Fund generally sells out-of-the-money 0DTE call options on the Innovation-100 Index that will expire at the end of the day.

Risks

Specific QDTE risks noted in their prospectus:

Additionally, the Fund is a “synthetic” covered call strategy, meaning that it derives its long exposure to the Innovation-100 Index from options that utilize the Innovation-100 Index as the reference asset. This synthetic exposure increases the likelihood that the Fund’s returns may not always precisely align with the returns of the Innovation-100 Index.

Key risks for both, as outlined in the prospectus:

Covered Call Strategy Risk. A covered call strategy involves writing (selling) covered call options in return for the receipt of premiums. The seller of the option gives up the opportunity to benefit from price increases in the underlying instrument above the exercise price of the options, but continues to bear the risk of underlying instrument price declines. The premiums received from the options may not be sufficient to offset any losses sustained from underlying instrument price declines. Exchanges may suspend the trading of options during periods of abnormal market volatility. Suspension of trading may mean that an option seller is unable to sell options at a time that may be desirable or advantageous to do so.

Conclusion

Both funds are solid within their category. They track their benchmark indexes closely in total‑return terms while providing high income. They aren’t directly comparable, since one is based on the S&P 500 and the other on the Nasdaq‑100 — so the choice ultimately depends on which underlying index exposure an investor prefers.

If I had to pick a winner, I would lean toward QDTE due to the fund family’s longer operating history, larger AUM, and stronger liquidity, which together provide a greater sense of reliability for me.

That said, I initiate coverage on both funds with a Hold rating. These ratings can be seen as neutral, since both remain exposed to broad market risk due to their strong correlation with underlying indexes. My market outlook remains cautious given the ongoing geopolitical uncertainty in the Middle East.
